items.title: Metatarsophalangeal angle >15°
items.contentHtml:
Metatarsophalangeal angle greater than 15°

  • The normal metatarsophalangeal angle is <15°; Values >15° define hallux valgus.
  • It is measured on an anteroposterior weight-bearing radiograph, between the longitudinal axis of the proximal phalanx and the first metatarsal.
  • An angle >40° is considered a severe deformity and is usually associated with sesamoid subluxation and osteoarthritis. metatarsophalangeal.
  • The progression of the angle correlates with the intermetatarsal angle (normal <9°).
  • For the examination: remember that the diagnostic threshold is >15°; Not to be confused with the intermetatarsal angle which assesses the space between the first and second metatarsals.

items.title: female predominance
items.contentHtml:
Higher incidence in women

  • The prevalence of hallux valgus is 3-5 times higher in women than in men.
  • Contributing factors: use of narrow footwear with high heels (increases pressure in the forefoot), constitutional ligamentous laxity and biomechanical alterations such as flat foot.
  • The weakness of the ligament transverse metatarsal and insufficiency of the tendon of the peroneus longus muscle favor lateral deviation of the first metatarsal.
  • Examination trap: the higher incidence in women is not due to primary genetic difference, but to mechanical and shoe factors.

items.foco: Narrow footwear as a causal factor
items.title: Narrow footwear as a cause
items.contentHtml:
Narrow footwear as a causal factor

  • Narrow footwear with a narrow toe and high heel forces the toes into adduction, compressing the first metatarsal in varus.
  • This generates an increase in the metatarsophalangeal angle and the intermetatarsal angle due to muscle imbalance.
  • In populations that do not wear shoes (e.g. traditional Japan), the incidence of hallux valgus is almost zero.
  • Footwear is the main modifiable extrinsic factor; Its correction is part of the conservative treatment of first line.

items.foco: Lateral deviation of the first metatarsal
items.title: Lateral deviation of the first metatarsal
items.contentHtml:
Lateral deviation of the first metatarsal

  • The lateral deviation of the first metatarsal (varus metatarsal) is the primary deformity in hallux valgus; the big toe deviates laterally, but the metatarsal deviates medially.
  • It is quantified by the intermetatarsal angle (normal <9°; >15° indicates severe deformity).
  • The head of the first metatarsal becomes prominent on the medial aspect of the foot, generating a prominent medial bursa painful.
  • Muscular imbalance (tendon of the adductor hallucis muscle pulls laterally) perpetuates the deformity.

items.title: Sesamoid subluxation
items.contentHtml:
Sesamoid subluxation

  • The sesamoids (medial and lateral) are located under the head of the first metatarsal, within the tendon of the flexor hallucis brevis muscle.
  • In hallux valgus, the metatarsal head is displaced medially, while the sesamoids remain lateral, producing subluxation of the sesamoid.
  • In radiography, the lateral sesamoid is located in the intermetatarsal space; joint congruity is evaluated by the position of the sesamoids with respect to the metatarsal head.
  • Subluxation is a marker of severity and is associated with chronic metatarsophalangeal osteoarthritis.

items.title: Pain in medial bursa
items.contentHtml:
Pain in prominent medial bursa

  • The prominent medial bursa is formed by the friction of the head of the first metatarsal against the shoe, generating a painful bursitis.
  • Pain is the main symptom that motivates consultation; It worsens with narrow shoes and improves with wide shoes.
  • The bursa can become inflamed, infected (rare), or form a synovial cyst.
  • Conservative treatment includes orthoses (interdigital separators) and wide shoes; If the bursitis is severe, it can be aspirated or infiltrated with corticosteroids.

items.title: Joint congruence on X-ray
items.contentHtml:
Radiological sign of joint congruity

  • Joint congruity is evaluated at the metatarsophalangeal joint of the big toe on anteroposterior weight-bearing radiograph.
  • A congruent joint shows parallel articular surfaces without subluxation; incongruent indicates lateral subluxation of the proximal phalanx with respect to the metatarsal head.
  • congruity is key to deciding the surgical technique: in congruent joints Chevron osteotomy is preferred; in incongruent, Scarf osteotomy or metatarsophalangeal arthrodesis.
  • Trap: do not confuse congruity with metatarsophalangeal angle; A joint can have a high angle but be congruent.

items.title: Conservative treatment: orthosis
items.contentHtml:
Conservative treatment with orthoses

  • First line in mild to moderate deformity or in patients not candidates for surgery.
  • Includes: wide footwear (wide toe, low heel), interdigital separators (silicone orthosis), night splints to keep the toe aligned.
  • The orthoses do not correct the structural deformity, they only relieve symptoms and slow progression.
  • analgesia (topical or oral non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs) and ice are recommended for bursitis.
  • Physiotherapy with strengthening exercises for the adductor hallucis muscle can improve biomechanics.

items.title: Surgery according to intermetatarsal angle
items.contentHtml:
Surgery according to intermetatarsal angle

  • The surgical indication is based on the intermetatarsal angle (IMA) and the metatarsophalangeal angle.
  • For IMA <13° (mild deformity): Chevron osteotomy (distal osteotomy in V).
  • For AIM 13°-16° (moderate): Scarf osteotomy (Z diaphyseal osteotomy).
  • For AIM >16° or severe osteoarthritis: metatarsophalangeal arthrodesis (joint fusion) or technique Lapidus (arthrodesis of the cuneometatarsal joint).
  • The choice also considers articular congruity and the age of the patient.

items.title: Chevron: mild deformity
items.contentHtml:
Chevron technique for mild deformity

  • The Chevron osteotomy is a V-shaped osteotomy in the head of the first metatarsal, with lateral displacement of the head to correct the intermetatarsal angle.
  • Indicated in mild deformity (metatarsophalangeal angle <30°, intermetatarsal angle <13°) and congruent joint.
  • Advantages: intrinsic stability (due to the V shape), rapid consolidation, low complication rate.
  • Contraindicated in severe metatarsophalangeal osteoarthritis or severe sesamoid subluxation.
  • Trap: not to be confused with Akin osteotomy (osteotomy in the proximal phalanx, which is used as a complement).

items.title: Scarf: moderate deformity
items.contentHtml:
Scarf osteotomy for moderate

  • The Scarf osteotomy is a Z-diaphyseal osteotomy of the first metatarsal, which allows three-dimensional correction (lateral displacement, rotation and shortening).
  • Indicated in moderate deformity (metatarsophalangeal angle 30°-40°, intermetatarsal angle 13°-16°), especially with sesamoid subluxation.
  • Allows fixation with screws, offering high stability and early consolidation.
  • Complications: box syndrome (injury of the dorsal cutaneous nerve), pseudarthrosis and recurrence if the rotational component is not corrected.
  • It can be combined with Akin osteotomy to correct the deviation of the phalanx.

items.title: MTP arthrodesis: severe
items.contentHtml:
Metatarsophalangeal arthrodesis in severe

  • metatarsophalangeal arthrodesis (fusion of the joint between the first metatarsal and the proximal phalanx) is the treatment of choice in severe deformity (metatarsophalangeal angle >40°, intermetatarsal angle >16°), advanced osteoarthritis or recurrence after previous surgery.
  • It is fixed with plates and screws, achieving a fusion in 10°-15° of valgus and 10°-15° of dorsiflexion.
  • It provides pain relief and stable correction, but eliminates joint mobility.
  • Contraindicated in active infection, severe peripheral vascular disease or Charcot neuroarthropathy.
  • Trap: in young active patients Scarf osteotomy is preferred if possible; Arthrodesis is reserved for people over 50 years of age or osteoarthritis.
